sections.title: Section 0: Onboarding Guide
sections.html:
CANONICAL LESSON · BUILD BEFORE DEEP DIVE

Mission

Run one Luau instruction, observe its result, and explain where that result appears.

01MENTAL MODEL

Instructions become visible evidence

A script is a set of instructions. Luau runs those instructions in order; print is a simple way to make one result visible while you learn.

In Roblox Studio, scripts live in the DataModel. In LuauX Practice, the browser sandbox gives you a safe place to test language ideas. It is useful for learning Luau, but it is not a full Roblox engine simulation.

02BUILD

Send your first signal

  1. Open Practice and run the code exactly once.
  2. Change only the text between the quotation marks, then run it again.
  3. Say out loud what changed: the instruction stayed the same; the string value changed.
local message = "LuauX online"
print(message)
03VERIFY

Prove the result

  • The output contains LuauX online exactly once.
  • After changing the string, the output changes without changing the print instruction.
  • You can point to message as a name and "LuauX online" as a string value.
04DEBUG

A name is not text until you quote it

What you see: The sandbox reports that it does not know what LuauX is.

print(LuauX online)

Why it happens: Without quotation marks, Luau reads LuauX and online as names it should resolve. Put text inside quotation marks so it is a string.

05EXPLAIN

Why this works

local creates a local binding named message. print receives the value stored in that binding and writes a representation to the output. The same pattern—store state, pass it to a function, observe the result—will recur throughout the course.

06EXTEND

Go further when the core is solid

In Studio, create a Script in ServerScriptService, open Output, then run a playtest. Compare that Studio output with the browser sandbox: both teach you to observe code, but only Studio runs against a Roblox place.

VERIFY THE MODEL
Roblox documented Roblox: Create a script ↗Roblox documented Roblox: Scripting overview ↗

sections.title: Section 1: Lexical Grammar
sections.html:
CANONICAL LESSON · BUILD BEFORE DEEP DIVE

Mission

Read a short Luau instruction accurately enough to predict what is a name, a value, and punctuation.

01MENTAL MODEL

Code is structured text

Luau reads characters using rules. Names identify values or functions; punctuation groups an instruction; quoted characters form a string.

You do not need compiler internals to debug a first script. Start by checking spelling, quotation marks, parentheses, and the exact name you intended to use.

02BUILD

Label a print instruction

  1. Run the code.
  2. Change only one token at a time: the name, the punctuation, or the string.
  3. Write down which change created a syntax error and which created a name error.
local playerName = "Ari"
print("Welcome, " .. playerName)
03VERIFY

Prove the result

  • The output is Welcome, Ari.
  • You can identify playerName as a name and "Welcome, " as a string.
04DEBUG

Capital letters change a name

What you see: Luau cannot find PlayerName.

local playerName = "Ari"
print(PlayerName)

Why it happens: Luau names are case-sensitive. PlayerName and playerName are different names.

05EXPLAIN

Why this works

Code is not guessed from intent. The parser needs the punctuation to be in the right places, and name lookup needs the spelling and capitalization to match the binding you created.

06EXTEND

Go further when the core is solid

Open the Luau grammar after you can reliably read small instructions. Treat it as a reference, not the first way to learn to script.

VERIFY THE MODEL
Luau documented Luau syntax reference ↗Luau documented Luau grammar ↗

sections.title: Section 2: Variable Bindings
sections.html:
CANONICAL LESSON · BUILD BEFORE DEEP DIVE

Mission

Store and update local game state without leaking a name outside the scope where it belongs.

01MENTAL MODEL

A local name has a home

A variable binding gives a value a name. local keeps that name in the block where you declared it, which makes dependencies easier to see and prevents accidental shared state.

A value can change while its name stays useful. What matters is which binding your code can currently see.

02BUILD

Track one coin pickup

  1. Run the code, then change the starting number and predict the final output before running again.
  2. Add another local named pickupAmount and use it instead of the literal 5.
  3. Keep both names local; do not create a global just to share a value.
local coins = 25
local pickupAmount = 5

coins += pickupAmount
print("Coins: " .. coins)
03VERIFY

Prove the result

  • The initial values 25 and 5 print Coins: 30.
  • Changing pickupAmount changes the result predictably.
  • The code uses local for both pieces of state.
04DEBUG

A branch-local name cannot escape the branch

What you see: The second print cannot find bonus.

if true then
    local bonus = 10
    print(bonus)
end

print(bonus)

Why it happens: bonus exists only inside the if block. If later code needs a value, declare the outer binding before the branch and assign it inside the branch.

05EXPLAIN

Why this works

The assignment operator updates the existing coins binding. A block can read names from an outer scope, but a local declared inside the block is not available after end. This is a readability and correctness tool first—not a reason to chase micro-optimizations.

06EXTEND

Go further when the core is solid

Turn the pickup into a function that receives the current count and returns the next count. That prepares you for the state-and-function patterns in the next units.

VERIFY THE MODEL
Roblox documented Roblox: Scripting basics ↗Luau documented Luau syntax reference ↗

sections.title: Section 3: Expression Precedence
sections.html:
CANONICAL LESSON · BUILD BEFORE DEEP DIVE

Mission

Combine values in the intended order and make an expression readable enough to verify.

01MENTAL MODEL

An expression produces one value

Operators combine values. Parentheses make the order explicit when a human reader could reasonably misunderstand it.

Use numbers for arithmetic and .. for string concatenation. A quoted number is text, not a number you can safely add.

02BUILD

Calculate a round reward

  1. Run the reward calculation.
  2. Change multiplier and bonus separately and predict the result.
  3. Add parentheses around the multiplication so the intended order is visible.
local baseReward = 20
local multiplier = 2
local bonus = 5
local reward = (baseReward * multiplier) + bonus
print("Reward: " .. reward)
03VERIFY

Prove the result

  • The initial output is Reward: 45.
  • Changing bonus does not change the multiplication result.
04DEBUG

Text cannot do arithmetic

What you see: Arithmetic reports a string value.

local reward = "20" + 5

Why it happens: Quotation marks make 20 a string. Keep arithmetic values as numbers; convert to text only when presenting output.

05EXPLAIN

Why this works

The parentheses calculate baseReward times multiplier first. The resulting number is then added to bonus. Concatenation happens only when the final number is presented beside text.

06EXTEND

Go further when the core is solid

Use a conditional expression to clamp a reward to a minimum, then compare a readable two-step calculation with a dense one-line expression.

VERIFY THE MODEL
Luau documented Luau syntax reference ↗

sections.title: Section 4: Boolean Logic
sections.html:
CANONICAL LESSON · BUILD BEFORE DEEP DIVE

Mission

Write a clear gameplay gate using Luau truthiness and short-circuit logic.

01MENTAL MODEL

Only false and nil stop the gate

In Luau, only false and nil are falsy. Zero, an empty string, and an empty table are still truthy, so do not use them as if they mean no.

and and or evaluate left to right and may stop early. They return one of their operand values, not necessarily the boolean words true or false.

02BUILD

Gate a locked door

  1. Run the code with hasKey set to false, then set it to true.
  2. Add a second flag called isDoorPowered and require both conditions.
  3. Test with zero and explain why zero does not behave like false in Luau.
local hasKey = false
local isDoorPowered = true
local canOpen = hasKey and isDoorPowered

if canOpen then
    print("Door opened")
else
    print("Find the key first")
end
03VERIFY

Prove the result

  • false and true prints Find the key first.
  • true and true prints Door opened.
  • You can explain why 0 is not a substitute for false.
04DEBUG

The old and-or shortcut loses false

What you see: The expression returns the fallback even when hasAccess is true.

local isHidden = hasAccess and false or true

Why it happens: When hasAccess is true, the and expression produces false, so or continues to true. Use a Luau if expression when either intended result can be false or nil.

05EXPLAIN

Why this works

Short-circuiting is useful because the right side is not evaluated when the left side already determines the result. Use it for readable guards, but use an if expression when you are choosing between values and either result may be falsy.

06EXTEND

Go further when the core is solid

Rewrite the door state as an if expression: local message = if canOpen then "Door opened" else "Find the key first". Compare it with the statement form.

VERIFY THE MODEL
Luau documented Luau syntax: if expressions ↗Luau documented Luau grammar ↗

sections.title: Section 5: Conditional Statements
sections.html:
CANONICAL LESSON · BUILD BEFORE DEEP DIVE

Mission

Choose one gameplay outcome with an if statement, then use an if expression when a value is required.

01MENTAL MODEL

A conditional chooses a path

An if statement chooses which block of instructions runs. Each branch has its own local scope, so temporary work stays close to the decision that needs it.

An if expression chooses a value. It requires an else because it must always produce one result.

02BUILD

Classify a player state

  1. Run the code with health set to 40, then 0, then -5.
  2. Add an elseif branch for low health below 20.
  3. Use the returned status in a second print so you can see the value separately from the decision.
local health = 40

local status = if health <= 0 then "Respawning" else "Active"

if health <= 0 then
    print("Disable player input")
else
    print("Keep playing")
end

print("Status: " .. status)
03VERIFY

Prove the result

  • health 40 prints Keep playing and Status: Active.
  • health 0 prints Disable player input and Status: Respawning.
  • Your elseif branch has a condition and the whole statement still ends once.
04DEBUG

elseif is one word

What you see: The parser asks for another end or the branches nest in an unexpected way.

if health <= 0 then
    print("Respawning")
else if health < 20 then
    print("Low health")
end

Why it happens: else if starts a new if inside else, so it needs another end. Use elseif for one decision chain, or deliberately add the second end for a nested decision.

05EXPLAIN

Why this works

Statements control which instructions happen. Expressions produce a value that can be assigned. Keeping temporary locals inside the branch makes it clear which outcome owns them and prevents later code from depending on a value that was never created.

06EXTEND

Go further when the core is solid

Create a three-state status with an if expression: Respawning, Low health, or Active. Then decide which part should remain a statement because it performs an action rather than calculates a value.

VERIFY THE MODEL
Luau documented Luau syntax: if expressions ↗Luau documented Luau grammar: conditional statements ↗

sections.title: Section 6: Iterative Loops
sections.html:
CANONICAL LESSON · BUILD BEFORE DEEP DIVE

Mission

Use a bounded loop to repeat a deliberate task without creating an accidental infinite loop.

01MENTAL MODEL

A loop needs a reason to stop

A loop repeats a block. A numeric for loop is ideal when you know the number of repetitions; a while loop is for a condition that can change.

Before running a loop, state what changes and what eventually stops it.

02BUILD

Spawn three practice rewards

  1. Run the numeric loop.
  2. Change the upper bound from 3 to 5 and predict the number of lines.
  3. Add the loop index to the printed message.
for rewardNumber = 1, 3 do
    print("Spawn reward #" .. rewardNumber)
end
03VERIFY

Prove the result

  • The output has exactly three lines at the initial bound.
  • The final line includes reward #3.
04DEBUG

A while loop must change its condition

What you see: The script keeps printing and never reaches the next line.

local count = 0
while count < 3 do
    print(count)
end

Why it happens: count never changes, so count < 3 remains true. Increment count inside the loop or use a numeric for loop when the bounds are known.

05EXPLAIN

Why this works

The numeric for loop owns its counter and endpoint. This makes the stop condition visible in one place and avoids a common class of accidental endless work.

06EXTEND

Go further when the core is solid

Use continue to skip one loop iteration, then decide whether a filtering condition would be clearer before the loop instead.

VERIFY THE MODEL
Luau documented Luau grammar: for and while ↗

sections.title: Section 7: Generic Loops
sections.html:
CANONICAL LESSON · BUILD BEFORE DEEP DIVE

Mission

Walk through a collection and use the value you actually need.

01MENTAL MODEL

Collections have positions and values

An array stores ordered values. A for-in loop can give you both an index and a value, so choose names that reveal which one you use.

Iteration is not magic: each turn of the loop binds the next result before running the body.

02BUILD

Read a pickup list

  1. Run the loop.
  2. Add a fourth pickup.
  3. Change the print to include both index and pickup.
local pickups = {"Coin", "Gem", "Key"}

for index, pickup in pickups do
    print(index .. ": " .. pickup)
end
03VERIFY

Prove the result

  • Each pickup appears once in the output.
  • The index and the pickup are not confused.
04DEBUG

Use the value, not the numeric position

What you see: The UI prints 1, 2, 3 instead of the item names.

for index, pickup in pickups do
    print(index)
end

Why it happens: index is the position. pickup is the value stored at that position. Choose the binding that matches what the output needs.

05EXPLAIN

Why this works

The loop creates fresh local bindings for each result. Naming them index and pickup makes the data shape visible and reduces accidental use of the wrong variable.

06EXTEND

Go further when the core is solid

Compare an ordered array with a dictionary of named settings, then choose an iteration shape that communicates the data you own.

VERIFY THE MODEL
Luau documented Luau syntax: generalized iteration ↗Luau documented Luau grammar ↗

sections.title: Section 8: Generalized Iteration
sections.html:
CANONICAL LESSON · BUILD BEFORE DEEP DIVE

Mission

Choose direct generalized iteration for a table before reaching for older iterator helpers.

01MENTAL MODEL

The collection can provide the next result

Modern Luau supports generalized iteration, so a table can be used directly in a for-in loop.

The point is not memorizing a clever shorthand. The point is to make it obvious what collection you are traversing and what each loop receives.

02BUILD

Inspect named settings

  1. Run the loop.
  2. Add one setting and predict its key/value output.
  3. Replace the names with a game system you recognize.
local settings = {music = true, volume = 0.7, difficulty = "normal"}

for key, value in settings do
    print(key .. ": " .. tostring(value))
end
03VERIFY

Prove the result

  • Every named setting prints a key and a value.
  • You use tostring only to present non-string values in text.
04DEBUG

A table value may not concatenate as text

What you see: Concatenation reports a non-string value.

print("Volume: " .. settings.volume)

Why it happens: settings.volume is a number. Convert it deliberately for output with tostring, while keeping it numeric for calculations.

05EXPLAIN

Why this works

Generalized iteration makes the table the source of results. The key/value names are local to each loop turn, so they should describe the shape of the collection rather than the implementation detail.

06EXTEND

Go further when the core is solid

Read the generalized-iteration syntax notes, then compare it with a numeric loop over an array and explain which one preserves the intended order.

VERIFY THE MODEL
Luau documented Luau syntax: generalized iteration ↗

sections.title: Section 9: Functions Parameters
sections.html:
CANONICAL LESSON · BUILD BEFORE DEEP DIVE

Mission

Write a small function that receives state, returns the next state, and makes its effect easy to test.

01MENTAL MODEL

A function is a named contract

A function names a repeatable piece of work. Parameters are the inputs it receives; return values are the results it sends back.

Prefer returning the next value instead of silently changing distant state. It makes a function easier to test, reuse, and move into a ModuleScript later.

02BUILD

Award a coin pickup

  1. Run the code and predict the output before you do.
  2. Call awardCoins with a different pickup amount.
  3. Add a guard so a negative pickup does not reduce coins.
local function awardCoins(currentCoins: number, pickupAmount: number): number
    return currentCoins + pickupAmount
end

local coins = 25
coins = awardCoins(coins, 5)
print("Coins: " .. coins)
03VERIFY

Prove the result

  • The initial call prints Coins: 30.
  • Changing the second argument changes only the amount awarded.
  • You assign the returned value back into coins.
04DEBUG

Returning a value does not update a caller by itself

What you see: The function runs, but coins still prints 25.

local coins = 25
awardCoins(coins, 5)
print(coins)

Why it happens: awardCoins returns a new number. Store that return value: coins = awardCoins(coins, 5).

05EXPLAIN

Why this works

Function parameters are local bindings for one call. Numbers are values, so assigning a new number inside the function does not change the caller's binding. Returning the next value makes the state transition explicit. The annotations help the type checker describe the intended inputs and result; they do not convert values at runtime.

06EXTEND

Go further when the core is solid

Make a table-based player state and return a new result deliberately. Then compare dot and colon method syntax only after you understand the explicit parameter form.

VERIFY THE MODEL
Roblox documented Roblox: Scripting basics ↗Luau documented Luau syntax: type annotations ↗

sections.title: Section 10: Closures Upvalues
sections.html:
CANONICAL LESSON · BUILD BEFORE DEEP DIVE

Mission

Use an outer local deliberately inside a function and recognize when a closure keeps that state available.

01MENTAL MODEL

A function can remember an outer binding

A function declared inside another scope can read a local from the outer scope. That remembered relationship is called a closure.

This is useful for small, owned state. Do not use it to hide state that another part of the game must understand or validate.

02BUILD

Make a local score counter

  1. Run the counter twice.
  2. Change the increment.
  3. Explain which local stays available between calls.
local score = 0

local function addScore(amount)
    score += amount
    return score
end

print(addScore(10))
print(addScore(5))
03VERIFY

Prove the result

  • The outputs are 10 then 15.
  • You can identify score as the outer state used by addScore.
04DEBUG

A new local resets the state

What you see: Every call returns the same small value.

local function addScore(amount)
    local score = 0
    score += amount
    return score
end

Why it happens: score is recreated for every call. Put the owned state in the intended outer scope, or pass it in and return the next value when shared state should stay explicit.

05EXPLAIN

Why this works

The function closes over the outer score binding. That gives the counter memory between calls, but it also creates a dependency you should name and keep small.

06EXTEND

Go further when the core is solid

Refactor the counter to accept current score and return next score. Compare explicit state with closure-owned state and choose based on who needs to own the truth.

VERIFY THE MODEL
Roblox documented Roblox: Scripting basics ↗Luau documented Luau grammar: local functions ↗

sections.title: Section 11: Call Stack
sections.html:
CANONICAL LESSON · BUILD BEFORE DEEP DIVE

Mission

Choose a loop for repeated gameplay work and recognize recursion as a controlled, smaller-problem technique.

01MENTAL MODEL

Every call needs a return path

A function call creates work that must finish before the caller continues. Recursion is a function calling itself with a smaller problem.

In game scripts, a loop is usually clearer for repeating over a known collection. Use recursion only when the structure itself is recursive and you can state the base case.

02BUILD

Count down with a base case

  1. Run the function with 3.
  2. Change the base case to stop at 1.
  3. Explain which call prevents the next call.
local function countdown(value)
    if value <= 0 then
        print("Go!")
        return
    end

    print(value)
    countdown(value - 1)
end

countdown(3)
03VERIFY

Prove the result

  • The output is 3, 2, 1, Go!.
  • You can point to the base case and the smaller next call.
04DEBUG

A recursive call must move toward its base case

What you see: The function never reaches Go!.

countdown(value)

Why it happens: Passing the same value repeats the same problem. Reduce the argument or choose a loop if the work is simply a known number of repetitions.

05EXPLAIN

Why this works

The base case returns without another call. Each other call receives a smaller value, so the chain eventually reaches that return path.

06EXTEND

Go further when the core is solid

Trace a nested table search on paper before coding it. If the structure is flat, prefer a loop and explain why it is easier to inspect.

VERIFY THE MODEL
Luau documented Luau grammar: function calls and conditionals ↗

sections.title: Section 12: Table Layouts
sections.html:
CANONICAL LESSON · BUILD BEFORE DEEP DIVE

Mission

Create an ordered table, update one item, and avoid assuming the length operator proves a table has no gaps.

01MENTAL MODEL

An array is an ordered table convention

Luau tables can hold values at numeric keys. When you use consecutive numeric positions starting at 1, you are using the common array convention.

The length operator is useful for dense arrays, but a table with missing numeric positions is not a reliable place to guess from # alone.

02BUILD

Equip a three-slot inventory

  1. Run the code.
  2. Replace the second item.
  3. Append one item with table.insert.
local inventory = {"Sword", "Potion", "Key"}
inventory[2] = "Shield"
table.insert(inventory, "Map")

print(inventory[2])
print(#inventory)
03VERIFY

Prove the result

  • The second item is Shield.
  • The dense inventory length is 4 after insertion.
04DEBUG

Tables start their normal array positions at 1

What you see: The first inventory lookup returns nil.

print(inventory[0])

Why it happens: The array convention uses 1 for the first element. Use inventory[1] for the first item and keep ordered numeric keys dense.

05EXPLAIN

Why this works

A table is flexible; the array behavior comes from the keys you choose. Keeping inventory entries consecutive makes indexing, insertion, and length checks predictable.

06EXTEND

Go further when the core is solid

Create a sparse table intentionally and observe why # is no longer a trustworthy count. Then decide whether a dictionary key would communicate the model better.

VERIFY THE MODEL
Roblox documented Roblox: Scripting basics ↗Luau documented Luau grammar: table constructors ↗

sections.title: Section 13: Table Dictionaries
sections.html:
CANONICAL LESSON · BUILD BEFORE DEEP DIVE

Mission

Use named table keys for state where a position would hide the meaning.

01MENTAL MODEL

A dictionary answers by name

A dictionary-style table maps meaningful keys to values. Use it for named settings, player state, or lookup data where position is not the important fact.

Reading a missing key produces nil, so guard optional data before using it as if it exists.

02BUILD

Read a player state record

  1. Run the code.
  2. Add a level field.
  3. Use a conditional to handle a missing title safely.
local playerState = {coins = 30, title = "Rookie"}

print(playerState.coins)
print(playerState.title)
03VERIFY

Prove the result

  • coins prints 30 and title prints Rookie.
  • A missing key is checked before concatenation or property-style use.
04DEBUG

A missing key is nil

What you see: Concatenation fails after reading a title that was never set.

print("Title: " .. playerState.rank)

Why it happens: rank is nil because the table has no rank key. Supply a fallback after checking the value, or ensure the state record creates the key.

05EXPLAIN

Why this works

Dot syntax is a readable form for string-like keys. The important decision is semantic: coins communicates a fact directly, while playerState[1] would force every reader to remember an unwritten convention.

06EXTEND

Go further when the core is solid

Nest a small settings table, then decide where a separate ModuleScript becomes clearer than one growing record.

VERIFY THE MODEL
Roblox documented Roblox: Scripting basics ↗Luau documented Luau grammar: table fields ↗

sections.title: Section 14: Math Library
sections.html:
CANONICAL LESSON · BUILD BEFORE DEEP DIVE

Mission

Use math helpers to keep a gameplay value inside an intentional range.

01MENTAL MODEL

A range is a rule you can enforce

Gameplay values often need boundaries: health should not drop below zero, volume should stay between zero and one, and a random reward should come from a stated range.

math.clamp expresses a boundary directly. It is clearer than scattering separate lower and upper checks through a system.

02BUILD

Clamp player health

  1. Run the code with health below zero.
  2. Test a health value above maxHealth.
  3. Change maxHealth and predict the result.
local health = -15
local maxHealth = 100
health = math.clamp(health, 0, maxHealth)
print("Health: " .. health)
03VERIFY

Prove the result

  • -15 becomes 0.
  • A value above maxHealth becomes maxHealth.
04DEBUG

Clamp arguments have an order

What you see: The result does not use the value you expected.

math.clamp(0, maxHealth, health)

Why it happens: math.clamp takes value, minimum, maximum. Put the state you are constraining first, then its allowed range.

05EXPLAIN

Why this works

The function returns a value inside the inclusive minimum/maximum range. Assigning that result back to health makes the state rule explicit at the point it changes.

06EXTEND

Go further when the core is solid

Use math.random for a clearly bounded practice reward, then separate randomness from any paid or fairness-sensitive game economy design.

VERIFY THE MODEL
Luau documented Luau syntax reference ↗Roblox documented Roblox: Scripting overview ↗

sections.title: Section 15: Table Library
sections.html:
CANONICAL LESSON · BUILD BEFORE DEEP DIVE

Mission

Use a table helper to update a collection intentionally and verify the collection before and after.

01MENTAL MODEL

A helper changes one collection rule

The table library provides operations such as insert, remove, clear, and clone. Use the helper that states the change you want instead of rebuilding a collection through accidental side effects.

When a collection represents game state, check the before and after values so a removal or insertion is observable.

02BUILD

Consume one inventory item

  1. Run the code.
  2. Remove a different position.
  3. Print the remaining items with a loop.
local inventory = {"Potion", "Key", "Map"}
local usedItem = table.remove(inventory, 1)

print("Used: " .. usedItem)
print("Remaining: " .. #inventory)
03VERIFY

Prove the result

  • usedItem is Potion.
  • The dense inventory has two remaining items.
04DEBUG

Removing changes later numeric positions

What you see: The item you expected at index 2 is now somewhere else.

table.remove(inventory, 1)
print(inventory[2])

Why it happens: Dense arrays shift later items left after a removal. Re-read the collection or use named keys when identity matters more than position.

05EXPLAIN

Why this works

table.remove returns the removed value and updates the dense numeric sequence. Capturing that return makes the consumed item explicit instead of losing it during the mutation.

06EXTEND

Go further when the core is solid

Use table.clone to compare a draft collection with an original, then verify what is and is not copied before treating it as an independent deep state copy.

VERIFY THE MODEL
Roblox documented Roblox: Scripting overview ↗Luau documented Luau syntax reference ↗

sections.title: Section 16: String Library
sections.html:
Section 16: The String Library (`string`)

The Mental Model: The Stone Tablet Library

To understand how Luau handles text, imagine a library where every single book is carved into a permanent stone tablet.

1. Stone Tablets Are Permanent (Immutability)

In Luau, once a string is written, it can never be altered. If you want to change the word "Cat" to "Cats", you cannot simply carve an "s" onto the end of the existing tablet. Instead, you must carve an entirely new tablet containing the word "Cats". The old "Cat" tablet remains unchanged in the library until it is no longer needed.

2. The Master Index (String Interning)

To save space, the library has a strict rule: only one copy of any unique word is allowed to exist.

  • If you ask the librarian for the word "Apple", they will look in a master index.
  • If "Apple" has already been carved, they will hand you a slip of paper pointing directly to that existing tablet.
  • This means comparing two words to see if they are identical is incredibly fast. The librarian doesn't need to read both tablets letter-by-letter. They just check if both slips of paper point to the exact same shelf location.

3. Mad Libs (Formatting)

string.format is like playing a game of Mad Libs. You provide a template with blank spaces (like %d for a number or %s for a word), and you hand the librarian the list of items to write into those blanks. This lets you construct clean, dynamic sentences without manually carving new words together step-by-step.

3.1 The Backtick Shortcut (String Interpolation)

Carving full sentences with string.format can get verbose. Luau offers a shortcut: backtick string interpolation. Instead of double quotes, wrap your text in backticks ( ). You can write variables directly inside the text inside curly braces {}. The librarian will read these braces, look up the variable, and automatically carve its value into the final tablet! E.g., local message = Hello, {name}!`

4. Vacuum-Packing Data (Binary Pack/Unpack)

Sometimes you don't need text to be readable by humans; you just want to pack numbers and letters tightly into a suitcase to send over a network or save to a file. string.pack acts like a vacuum-sealer, packing your numbers and text into a dense, unreadable block of bytes. string.unpack is the reverse process, opening the seal to pull the original numbers and text back out.

5. The Copy-Machine Trap (Concatenation)

Imagine you want to build a sentence one word at a time in a loop. If you write:

sentence = sentence .. "word"

You are asking the librarian to take the existing tablet, copy all of its letters onto a brand-new tablet, add the new word at the end, and throw the old tablet away. If you do this 1,000 times, you will waste hours copying and throwing away 999 intermediate stone tablets.

  • The Fix: Instead, write all your words down on individual scraps of paper, put them in a bucket (table), and ask the librarian to join them all together onto a single stone tablet in one go (table.concat).

sections.title: Section 17: Pattern Matching
sections.html:
Section 17: Pattern Matching & Parsers

The Mental Model: The Strict Customs Inspector

Imagine you are a customs inspector in a busy shipping port. Every day, thousands of cargo crates (text strings) pass through your station. Your job is to check if these crates contain specific items, like "a passport containing exactly 5 digits" or "a word that starts with a capital letter."

You could hire an expensive team to open and catalog every single item (this is what complex, heavy Regular Expression engines do), but that slows down the port. Instead, you use a highly optimized checklist with special shorthand codes:

  • Shorthand Codes (Character Classes): Instead of writing "check for a, b, c... z", you use a single code: %l (lowercase letter). If you want to check for numbers, you use %d (digit).
  • The Inversion Key: If you capitalize your code, it means "opposite." So, while %d means "find a digit," %D means "find anything that is *not* a digit."
  • The Greedy vs. Polite Inspector (Quantifiers):
  • Greedy (* and +): This inspector is hungry to find the largest match possible. If you say "find everything between brackets <...>," and hand them <div>content</div>, they will look at the very first < and the very last > on the page, returning the entire line.
  • Polite/Non-Greedy (-): This inspector stops as soon as the minimal criteria is met. Handed the same line, they stop at the very first closed bracket, returning just <div>.

Luau’s pattern matching is designed to be a fast, lightweight inspector that works out of a tiny, portable clipboard without needing a massive warehouse of memory to do its job.

sections.title: Section 18: Buffer Library
sections.html:
Section 18: The Buffer Library (`buffer`)

The Mental Model: The Dry-Erase Grid vs. The Printing Press

When managing text or numbers in code, we often treat data as highly flexible, but under the hood, computer memory has strict rules. Think of Strings and Buffers like two different ways of writing down information:

  • Strings (The Printing Press): Imagine you want to write the word "CAT". Your computer commissions a printing press to stamp "CAT" onto a strip of paper. If you want to change it to "CAR", you cannot erase the "T". Instead, you must run the printing press again, stamp out a brand new strip of paper containing "CAR", and throw the old strip in the trash. Doing this hundreds of times a second creates a mountain of paper waste that your computer must constantly clean up.
  • Buffers (The Dry-Erase Grid): A buffer is like a pre-built plastic grid of dry-erase slots, where each slot is exactly large enough to hold a single number. If you want to change the number in slot #3, you simply wipe off the old number and write a new one directly into that slot. The grid size never changes, no paper is wasted, and no cleanup crew is ever needed. This is what we call a zero-allocation or mutable operation.

By using a buffer, you allocate a fixed-size workspace once, and then rapidly overwrite its slots over and over again without causing your computer to slow down or clean up waste.

sections.title: Section 19: Bitwise Library
sections.html:
Section 19: The Bitwise Library (`bit32`)

The Mental Model: The 32-Switch Control Board

Imagine a physical control board containing a row of exactly 32 light switches, numbered from left to right (0 to 31). Each switch can only be in one of two positions: ON (1) or OFF (0).

In Luau, numbers are normally complex decimal values, but when you use the bit32 library, the computer temporarily translates your number into this row of 32 light switches.

1. The Core Operations (Comparing Boards)

Suppose you have two control boards and want to combine them into a third board:

  • band (AND): You compare both boards. You only turn a switch ON on the new board if that exact switch is ON on *both* input boards. If either is off, the final switch is off.
  • bor (OR): You look at both boards. If *at least one* of the boards has a switch turned ON, you turn it ON on the final board.
  • bxor (Exclusive OR): You check if the two boards disagree. If one switch is ON and the other is OFF, you turn the final switch ON. If they are both ON or both OFF, you turn the final switch OFF.
  • bnot (NOT): You take a single board and flip every single switch to its exact opposite state.

2. Shifts and Rotations (Sliding the Switches)

  • lshift (Shift Left): You slide all the switches a certain number of spaces to the left. The switches on the far left fall off the board and are lost forever. The empty spaces left on the right are filled with OFF switches.
  • rshift (Shift Right): You slide all switches to the right. Switches on the far right fall off, and the empty spaces on the left are filled with OFF switches.
  • arshift (Arithmetic Shift Right): You slide switches to the right, but instead of automatically filling the empty left spaces with OFF switches, you clone whatever state the leftmost switch originally had. This is used to preserve whether a number is positive or negative.
  • lrotate / rrotate (Rotate): You slide the switches, but instead of letting them fall off the board, the switches that slide off one end run around to the other side and plug back into the empty spots. No data is lost!

3. Extracting and Replacing (Working with Sections)

Instead of flipping all 32 switches individually, you can focus on a small group.

  • extract is like taking a magnifying glass, looking at a specific window of switches (for example, switches 4 through 7), and reading their values as a smaller, independent number.
  • replace allows you to take a small custom pattern of switches and overwrite a specific section of your main board without disturbing the other switches around it.

sections.title: Section 20: Coroutine Library
sections.html:
Section 20: The Coroutine Library (`coroutine`)

The Single-Chef Kitchen (Cooperative Multitasking)

Imagine you are a single chef cooking a complex dinner. You have three recipes to prepare: baking bread, simmering soup, and chopping vegetables. Because you only have one set of hands (one CPU), you cannot physically do all three tasks at the exact same millisecond.

Instead of trying to do everything at once, you work cooperatively:

1. You mix the bread dough, put it in the oven, and pause (yield) your work on the bread while it bakes.

2. While the bread is baking, you switch to the soup, turn on the heat, and pause (yield) to let it simmer.

3. While the soup simmers, you chop the vegetables.

4. When the oven timer goes off, you resume your bread-making task right where you left off.

This is exactly how coroutines work. They are not true parallel threads (like hiring three different chefs); instead, they are a way for a single execution thread to smartly pause a task, work on something else, and pick up the original task later without losing its place.

The Lifecycle of a Task

Every coroutine goes through life stages like a recipe:

  • Suspended (In the Cookbook): The recipe is written down (created), but you haven't started cooking it yet, or you have paused mid-way through.
  • Running (On the Stove): You are actively working on this recipe right now.
  • Normal (Waiting on Another Task): You are currently working on a sub-recipe that this main recipe requested.
  • Dead (Served): The dish is finished (or you dropped it on the floor with an error). You cannot work on it anymore.

Passing Notes (Data Routing)

When you pause a task, you can pass messages back and forth:

  • When you start a recipe, you pass in the raw ingredients (Start Parameters).
  • When you pause the recipe to wait, you can hand off a status report (Yielding Outputs).
  • When you resume the recipe, you can hand it new tools or ingredients to keep going (Resume Inputs).

sections.title: Section 21: Utf8 Library
sections.html:
Section 21: The UTF-8 Library (`utf8`)

Mental Model: The Variable-Length Cargo Train

Imagine standard English text is like a train where every single cargo car is exactly 1 meter long. If you want to find the 5th car, you can just measure 5 meters down the track and you will be exactly at the start of it. This is how standard computer strings (ASCII) work: 1 character equals exactly 1 byte.

Now, imagine Unicode and international text (like emojis, Kanji, or Arabic script) as a specialized train where cars have different lengths. A standard letter is still 1 meter long, but a complex Chinese character might be 3 meters long, and a smiley face emoji might be 4 meters long.

If you try to find the 5th character by measuring exactly 5 meters (using standard byte-based operations), you might end up cutting right through the middle of a 4-meter emoji. This breaks the character, leaving you with useless, corrupted scrap metal (invalid byte sequences).

The <code>utf8</code> library is your smart train inspector. Instead of blindly measuring meters (bytes), it walks the train, identifies where each unique car actually starts and ends, and ensures you never slice a cargo car in half.

sections.title: Section 22: Debug Library
sections.html:
Section 22: The Debug Library (`debug`)

The Mental Model: The Detective's Clipboard and Stopwatch

Imagine your running code is like a busy office building. Each time a function is called, a new floor is built on top of the building (the Call Stack). Inside that room, local variables are stored and work is done. If that function calls another function, a new floor is built on top of *that* one.

The debug library is like a master detective with a clipboard and a stopwatch who can inspect this building while it is running:

1. The Clipboard (debug.info and debug.traceback):

  • debug.info is like asking a worker on the 3rd floor: *"What is your name? What file did you come from? How many packages (parameters) did you bring with you?"*
  • debug.traceback is like taking a quick elevator ride from the top floor down to the lobby, writing down a list of every single floor you passed to see exactly how you got to the top.

2. The Stopwatch (debug.profilebegin and debug.profileend):

  • This lets you place a "start timer" sticker at the beginning of a complex task (like sorting a massive pile of mail) and a "stop timer" sticker at the end. The engine reads these stickers and draws a neat timeline showing exactly how many milliseconds that task took.

sections.title: Section 23: Type Checker Modes
sections.html:
Section 23: Type Checker Modes

The Toy Store Inspection Analogy

Imagine you are running a factory that packs toy boxes. You want to make sure every box contains exactly what is written on the label before shipping it to customers.

Luau’s type checker acts as your quality control inspector, and you can set their inspection strictness using three different settings (modes) at the top of your scripts:

  • No-Check Mode (--!nocheck - The Blindfolded Inspector): The inspector only checks if the boxes are physically sealed and not broken (syntax/grammar check). They don't look inside. If a box labeled "Action Figure" actually contains a banana, they won't stop it.
  • Non-Strict Mode (--!nonstrict - The Casual Inspector): This is the default setting. The inspector looks inside but is very laid-back. If they see you trying to plug a hair dryer into a glass of water, they will scream (clear runtime-crashing mistakes). But if you put a toy car in a box without a label, they will shrug and say, "Could be anything!" and let it pass.
  • Strict Mode (--!strict - The Perfectionist Inspector): The inspector has a clipboard with exact blueprints for every single item. Every single toy must have a label, and every label must match its blueprint perfectly. If a toy car is missing a wheel, or if you try to add a custom spoiler to a toy car after it has been built, the inspector halts the assembly line immediately.

sections.title: Section 24: Constraint Solver
sections.html:
Section 24: Constraint Solver & Unification

The Puzzle-Solver Analogy

Imagine you are trying to solve a logic puzzle (like Sudoku or a riddle board). Instead of looking at each space and guessing the answer immediately, you first go through the puzzle and write down a list of "clues":

  • *Clue 1:* "The red house belongs to the painter."
  • *Clue 2:* "The painter owns a dog."
  • *Clue 3:* "The person in the blue house owns a cat."

At first, you don't know who lives where. But once you have collected all the clues, you sit down and look at how they connect. By combining Clue 1 and Clue 2, you suddenly deduce a new fact: "The red house has a dog."

This is exactly how Luau's type checker works.

  • Constraint Generation: When the computer reads your code, it doesn't immediately check if everything is correct. Instead, it acts like a detective collecting clues. When it sees local x = 5, it writes down a clue: *"The type of x must be a number."* When it sees local y = x, it writes down another clue: *"The type of y must be the same as the type of x."*
  • Unification (The Solver): Once it has gathered all these clues (constraints), it connects them like puzzle pieces. By combining the two clues, it instantly deduces that y is also a number.
  • Conflict Resolution: If you later write y = "hello", it generates a conflicting clue: *"The type of y must be a string."* The solver realizes that a single puzzle piece cannot be both a number and a string at the same time, so it sounds an alarm (a type warning).

sections.title: Section 25: Types Classes
sections.html:
Section 25: Types, Classes & Objects

Have you ever tried to put a square peg in a round hole? Or maybe accidentally poured salt instead of sugar into a cake? In programming, "types" help us avoid these kinds of mix-ups by giving labels or categories to different kinds of information. It's like organizing your toy box!

What are Types?

Imagine every piece of information in your program is like a toy. A "type" is a label you put on that toy to say what kind of toy it is.

  • Primitive Toys (Basic Building Blocks):
  • boolean: A light switch toy. It can only be "ON" (true) or "OFF" (false).
  • number: A counting block. It's always a quantity, like 10, 3.14, or -5.
  • string: A name tag. It's always text, like "Hello World" or "Player1".
  • Custom Toy Boxes (Tables):

Sometimes you need to group several toys together. That's a "table."

  • Specific Toy Box Blueprint (Structural Table Interface): Imagine you have a blueprint for a "Robot Arm" toy box. It *must* contain a "Hand" (number) and a "Wrist" (number). If another toy box, regardless of its name, *also* has a "Hand" and "Wrist" of the right type, it matches the "Robot Arm" blueprint. The blueprint defines the *shape* of the box's contents.
    •         -- A blueprint for a 'Point' box that always has an 'x' and a 'y' number
        type Point = { x: number, y: number }
  • General Storage Box (Indexed Dictionaries): This is like a general shelf where you can put many different items. Each item needs a label (a string) and a count (a number). You can add new items to the shelf whenever you want, as long as they follow the "label:count" rule.
    •         -- A general 'Dictionary' box where any text label can point to a number
        type Dictionary = { [string]: number }
  • Exporting Your Blueprints (Module Boundaries): If you design a blueprint inside a shared toy factory (ModuleScript), other toy designers won't be able to see or copy it unless you stick an export stamp on it. Prepending export type BlueprintName = ... makes it public. Other scripts can read it by requiring the module, e.g., local factory = require(path) local toy: factory.BlueprintName = ...

How Do We Check if Toys/Boxes Match?

There are two main ways the program checks if things fit together:

1. Checking by "Shape" (Structural Verification - for Tables):

This is like comparing two toy boxes. If one box has slots for "Head," "Body," and "Legs," and another box *also* has slots for "Head," "Body," and "Legs" (even if they're called "Robot" and "Doll"), they're considered compatible because their *structure* or *contents* match. The name of the box doesn't matter as much as what's inside.

2. Checking by "Family Name" (Nominal Verification - for Roblox Instances):

This is like identifying toys by their brand or family. A "Car" toy belongs to the "Vehicle" family. Even if a "Boat" toy has wheels *and* a motor, it's not a "Car" because it doesn't have the "Car" family name. In Roblox, a "Part" is a specific type of building block that belongs to the "BasePart" family, so anything expecting a "BasePart" will happily accept a "Part."

Common "Oops!" Moments:

  • Adding to a "Sealed" Toy Box: If you define a small toy box with only specific items inside, you can't just throw a new, unplanned item into it later without telling the program first.
  • Fix: Either make the box flexible (allow optional new items) or put all potential items in from the start.
  • Assuming a Toy's Family: If you grab a toy from a general pile and *assume* it's a "Car" because it looks like one, you might be wrong. The program will let you say, "I'm sure this is a Car!" but if it's actually a "Boat" when you try to drive it, it will crash!
  • Fix: Be careful when you tell the program to ignore its checks. Only do it if you are absolutely, 100% certain what the toy is.

sections.title: Section 26: Unions Intersections
sections.html:
Section 26: Unions, Intersections & Singletons

Imagine you're organizing a collection of items, but sometimes you need very specific rules about what goes where. This section talks about how to describe those rules clearly.

1. Unions: "This OR That"

Think of a special container that can hold either a piece of fruit or a number.

  • You can put an apple in it.
  • You can put the number 5 in it.
  • But you *cannot* put a shoe in it – that's not allowed!

This is like saying "I need an apple OR a number." If you have something that's optional, like a "phone number that you *might* provide," it's like saying "you can provide a number OR you can provide nothing."

2. Intersections: "This AND That"

Now, imagine you need a toy that is both red and round.

  • A red square toy won't work.
  • A blue round toy won't work.
  • You need a toy that *simultaneously* has *all* the characteristics you asked for.

This is how you combine features. If you describe an item as needing "wheels AND an engine AND a steering wheel," it must have all of those things together.

3. Singletons: "Exactly This"

Sometimes you need to be extremely specific. Imagine a traffic light that can only be "Red," "Yellow," or "Green." It can't be "Purple" or "Sparkling."

  • You can set the light to "Red."
  • You can change it to "Green."
  • But you *cannot* change it to "Blue" – that's not a valid option for this traffic light.

This is for when a variable can only hold one of a few *exact* values, like specific words or numbers, not just any word or any number.

Hidden Traps (Making sure things work as expected!)

  • Mixed Container Problem (Union Property Access Trap): If you have that special container that holds "either an apple OR an orange," you can only safely ask it questions that are true for *both* apples and oranges (e.g., "Is it fruit?"). You can't ask "Does it have a stem?" unless you first peek inside and say, "Ah, it's definitely an apple! *Now* I can ask about its stem." Otherwise, you might be asking about a stem on an orange (which doesn't have one).
  • Conflicting Instructions (Intersection Function Overload Resolving Trap): Imagine you have a list of instructions for a robot. One says, "If you see something shiny, pick it up." Another says, "If you see something small, ignore it." If the robot sees a shiny, small coin, which instruction does it follow? The system needs a clear way to decide which rule applies first. Sometimes, the order you write the rules matters for how the robot interprets things.

sections.title: Section 27: Generics Type Packs
sections.html:
Section 27: Generics & Type Packs

Imagine you have a magical container or a special tool.

Generics: The Smart Box

Instead of building a box specifically for "apples," and then a completely separate box for "oranges," you can build one "Smart Box." When you put an apple into it, it instantly becomes an "Apple Box" and will only let you put more apples in or take apples out. If you put a toy car in, it becomes a "Toy Car Box." The magic is that the *type* of item it holds is decided when you first use it, and it then strictly sticks to that type, preventing you from accidentally mixing apples and toy cars. This helps you avoid messes and confusion.

Type Packs: The adaptable Conveyor Belt

Now, imagine a conveyor belt that takes items in and sends items out. Sometimes you put one item on, sometimes three, sometimes five. A "Type Pack" is like telling your conveyor belt: "Whatever sequence of items you receive on the input side (could be one apple, or an apple and an orange, or an apple, an orange, and a banana), you must promise to handle that exact sequence, and then send out a sequence of items that *matches the original pattern* on the output side." It doesn't care how many items or what they are, just that it keeps the *sequence's structure* intact. This is super handy for processes that need to gracefully handle varying numbers of inputs and outputs without breaking.

Both Generics and Type Packs help you make flexible tools and processes that are also very reliable and predictable, ensuring that your items (data) are handled correctly.

sections.title: Section 28: Type Narrowing
sections.html:
Section 28: Type Narrowing & Refinement

Imagine you have a magical container that can hold different kinds of items – sometimes a fruit, sometimes a vegetable, or maybe even nothing at all! You don't always know what's inside just by looking at the container.

  • Broad Type (Union): When you first get the container, it's like a "mystery item" holder. It *could* be a banana (fruit), *or* it *could* be a carrot (vegetable), *or* it *could* be empty. This is its broad type – it covers all possibilities.
  • Narrowing: To use the item properly (e.g., peel a banana, chop a carrot), you need to know exactly what it is. If you peek inside and see a banana, you've "narrowed" your understanding. Now you know for sure it's a banana, not a carrot or an empty container.
  • Type Guards: How do you peek? You use special "checks."
  • Specific Type Check: If you have a scanner that tells you, "This is a banana!" – that's a type guard. Once the scanner confirms it's a banana, you can confidently use a banana peeler. You wouldn't try to peel a carrot with it!
  • "Is there anything?" Check: Sometimes, you just need to know if the container is empty or not. A simple check like "Is there *something* in here?" is a guard. If there is, you've narrowed it from "something or nothing" to "definitely something."
  • Feature Check: What if you know the container holds *either* a toy car *or* a toy plane? A toy car has "wheels," and a toy plane has "wings." You can check if the toy *has* wheels. If it does, you know it's a car. If not, it must be the plane. You're narrowing based on its "features."

Why is this important?

This helps your computer program act smarter and more safely. Instead of constantly guessing or being prepared for *anything*, it can confidently perform specific actions once it knows *exactly* what kind of item it's dealing with. It's like knowing you have a banana means you can use a banana peeler without worrying about trying to peel a carrot with it.

Gotchas (Traps):

  • Item Swap Trap: You looked in the container and saw a banana. You started to get your peeler ready. But then, someone *secretly swapped* the banana for a carrot! Your program still *thinks* it's a banana because of your earlier check, but it's not. If you then try to use the banana peeler, it won't work on the carrot. The computer forgets your "narrowed" knowledge if the item itself changes *after* you checked.
  • Delayed Check Trap: You looked in the container, saw a banana, and told a friend, "Hey, there's a banana in here!" But your friend is busy and will check later. In the meantime, someone *again swapped* the banana for a carrot. When your friend finally checks, they'll find a carrot, even though you told them it was a banana. The computer can't guarantee its knowledge across time, especially if other parts of the program might change things behind the scenes before your friend (or another part of the program) gets to act.

sections.title: Section 29: Type Casting
sections.html:
Section 29: Type Casting & Assertions

Understanding How Our Digital Helpers Sort Information

Imagine you have a very organized digital helper (your program) that processes lots of information. This helper likes to categorize everything: numbers, words (text), true/false statements, and various kinds of objects like a "Toy Car" or a "Building Block." These categories are what we call "types."

Sometimes, your helper needs a little guidance, or it's smart enough to figure things out on its own.

1. Telling Your Helper What Something Is (Type Assertion `::`)

Think of this as putting a specific label on a box. Let's say you have a box that's vaguely labeled "Stuff from the garage." You, however, know for a fact that this particular box contains "Car Parts." You can tell your helper, "This 'Stuff from the garage' box *is* actually 'Car Parts'."

  • How it works: You use a special double-colon :: to make this declaration.
    •     the-box = StuffFromGarage :: CarParts

You're essentially saying, "Hey helper, for my purposes, treat this as 'Car Parts', even if you initially thought it was just generic 'Stuff'."

  • The "Any" Trick: Sometimes your helper is very strict and won't let you label a "number box" as a "word box" directly. If you really, really want to bypass its strict rules, you can temporarily label it as "Any Kind of Stuff" first, and *then* label it as a "word box." It's like telling your helper, "Just accept this as 'Any' first, then you can see it as a 'word'."
    •     my-value = 42 (a number)
    my-text = my-value :: AnyKindofStuff :: WordBox

Big Warning! This doesn't change what's inside the box! If it was a number, it's still a number. You've just tricked your helper into *thinking* it's a word, which can lead to big problems later if you try to use it like one.

2. Your Helper Automatically Adjusting Information (Type Coercion)

Other times, your helper is smart enough to make small adjustments on its own without you having to explicitly tell it.

  • Numbers and Math: If you tell your helper to add "ten" (a word) to 5 (a number), it understands you mean the *number* 10 and does the math:
    •     print("ten" + 5) --> It figures out "ten" means 10, then adds it to 5, resulting in 15.
  • Numbers and Words Together: If you ask your helper to combine the phrase "My result is: " (a word) with 42 (a number), it automatically turns the 42 into the word "forty-two" so they can fit together:
    •     print("My result is: " .. 42) --> "My result is: 42"

3. Hidden Dangers and Tricky Spots

  • Misleading Labels: If you tell your helper, "This 'Stuff from the garage' box is 'Car Parts'," but it actually contains "Gardening Tools," your helper won't know until it tries to find a "spark plug" inside. It will then get confused and crash, even though you explicitly told it it was "Car Parts."
  • Solution: Always double-check at the last minute if something *really* is what you expect it to be before trying to use it.
  • Strict Comparisons: Your helper is very particular when comparing things. If you ask if the *word* "ten" is exactly the same as the *number* 10, it will say "no." It won't try to convert "ten" into a number for comparison; it sees them as fundamentally different things. It might even get frustrated and stop working if you try to say "is 'ten' smaller than 10?"
  • Solution: If you want to compare a word that looks like a number with an actual number, always convert the word to a number first using a special "to number" tool.

In short, your digital helper is smart, but it's also very literal. Sometimes you can give it hints, and sometimes it can figure things out. But you must be careful not to give it bad information or expect it to be a mind-reader when comparing different kinds of things.

sections.title: Section 30: Compilation Bytecode
sections.html:
Section 30: Compilation & Bytecode Generation

Imagine you have a complex recipe written in plain English, full of natural language and descriptive steps. To make sure a super-efficient robot chef can follow it perfectly and quickly, you wouldn't just give it the English recipe. Instead, you'd hire a highly skilled translator and organizer.

That's exactly what happens when you write code in Luau!

Here's the mental model:

1. Your Luau Code (The English Recipe): This is what you write. It's easy for humans to understand, with words, loops, and function calls.

2. The Translator & Organizer (The Compiler): This is a special program that takes your human-readable Luau code and turns it into something the Luau "robot chef" (called the Virtual Machine or VM) can understand directly. It does this in a few steps:

  • Breaking it Down (Lexer): First, it reads your recipe word by word, identifying each part: "chop," "onion," "2," "cups." It doesn't understand the meaning yet, just what each "word" is.
  • Understanding the Structure (Parser): Next, it takes those words and figures out the grammar and structure. It understands that "chop 2 cups of onion" is a complete instruction. It builds a mental "sentence structure" for your entire recipe.
  • Making it Super Efficient (Optimizer): Before giving it to the robot, our smart translator reviews the recipe structure.
  • If you wrote "add 1 + 1 teaspoon of salt," it would quietly change it to "add 2 teaspoons of salt" to save the robot a calculation step.
  • If you had a very simple sub-step like "mix lightly" that you used often, the translator might just write out those specific mixing steps directly into the main recipe instead of telling the robot to "go do the 'mix lightly' sub-recipe," saving the robot from jumping back and forth.
  • If you wrote a step that can never possibly be reached or used, the translator would just remove it.
  • Writing Robot Instructions (Bytecode Generator): Finally, the translator converts the perfectly optimized recipe into a series of very simple, numbered instructions that the robot chef can execute directly: "Instruction 1: Get onion. Instruction 2: Chop onion. Instruction 3: Add to bowl." This final set of instructions is called Bytecode.

3. The Robot's Instruction Manual (The Bytecode File): This is the file containing all the robot's instructions.

  • It has a cover page (Header) telling the robot what version of instructions this is.
  • It has a glossary (String Table) that lists all unique words (like "onion," "salt") used in the recipes, giving each a short code number (e.g., "onion" is 001). This way, the instructions can just say "use 001" instead of writing "onion" every time, saving space.
  • It has a list of all mini-recipes (Proto List) for specific tasks, like "how to make sauce" or "how to bake cake," each with its own detailed steps.
  • And it has the main recipe (Main Proto) that tells the robot which mini-recipes to follow and in what order to cook the whole meal.

Why bother with all this?

Just like our robot chef can follow precise, optimized instructions much faster than trying to understand a human recipe on the fly, the Luau VM executes pre-compiled Bytecode incredibly quickly.

Things to Watch Out For:

  • No live recipe changes!: In a busy restaurant (like a live Roblox game), you can't suddenly yell new recipe instructions to the chef in the middle of cooking (loadstring()). It's a security risk (what if the new instruction is bad?) and totally throws off the chef's efficiency. The chef only works from pre-approved, optimized recipes.
  • Recipe complexity limits: Each individual mini-recipe (function) can only be so long or use so many distinct ingredients/steps. If it gets too complex, the translator will tell you to break it down into smaller, simpler sub-recipes. This keeps things manageable and efficient.

sections.title: Section 31: Vm Registers
sections.html:
Section 31: VM Registers & Stack Machine Layout

Imagine your computer is like a busy office, and every time you run a piece of code (like a Luau function), it's like an office worker tackling a new task.

Your Desk (The Stack): To do a task, the worker needs a workspace – a desk. In computer terms, this temporary workspace for a function is called the "stack." It's where all the information related to that specific task will live while it's being processed.

Labeled Bins (Registers): Now, Luau is a very organized worker. Instead of just piling everything onto the desk, it has a set of specifically labeled bins or compartments directly on the desk. These labeled compartments are called Registers.

  • There's always a special bin ($R_0$) to remind the worker *which task they're currently doing*.
  • Other bins ($R_1, R_2$, etc.) are used for the *materials* you receive for your task (like input values).
  • Even more bins are for *things you create* during the task (like new variables or calculations you make).

Your Task's Starting Point (Frame Pointer): When a new task begins, the worker mentally marks a specific spot on the desk as the "start of *my* current task's stuff." This helps them quickly find all their labeled bins without confusion. This marker is like the "Frame Pointer." All the bins are numbered relative to this starting point.

Direct Access - The Speedy Advantage: The clever part is that when Luau needs information from "Bin #3," it goes *directly* to Bin #3. It doesn't have to rummage through a pile of papers. This direct access makes Luau super fast, like a chef who knows exactly where every ingredient is in their organized kitchen, instead of digging through a messy pantry every time.

A Limited Number of Bins: Just like a physical desk has limited space, a Luau function only has a certain number of these labeled bins available for its task. If a task becomes too complex, needs too many separate pieces of information at once, or creates too many temporary items, it might run out of bins!

sections.title: Section 32: Opcodes
sections.html:
Section 32: VM Opcode Reference

Imagine you have a highly efficient robot chef who can cook any dish you give it. This robot chef doesn't understand human recipes directly; instead, it needs very specific, tiny instructions, almost like a simplified secret language. This "secret language" is what we call VM Opcodes.

Think of the Luau Virtual Machine (VM) as this robot chef. It has:

  • A Recipe Book (Bytecode Stream): This is a long list of all the tiny instructions (opcodes) it needs to follow to run your Luau code.
  • Tiny Containers on the Counter (Registers): These are small, labeled slots on the chef's counter, like "Container 1," "Container 2," etc. The chef uses them to hold ingredients temporarily while working.
  • A Pantry of Pre-made Ingredients (Constant Table): This is where the chef stores common ingredients that don't change, like "sugar," "salt," or specific numbers like "10."
  • A Note Pad for Extra Details (aux word): Sometimes, an instruction is too complex to fit on one line of the recipe. So, a separate note might be attached, providing extra details (like "use exactly 32 grams of flour from the *special* pantry shelf").

Every single instruction (opcode) is like a tiny command for the robot chef. These commands are super compact, like a coded message:

  • Instruction Formats: Just different ways the command is written:
  • Format A: "Do something with Container A." (e.g., LOP_LOADNIL R(A): "Put 'nothing' into Container A.")
  • Format AB: "Do something involving Container A and Container B." (e.g., LOP_MOVE R(A), R(B): "Take whatever is in Container B and put it into Container A.")
  • Format ABC: "Do something involving Containers A, B, and C." (e.g., LOP_ADD R(A), R(B), R(C): "Add the contents of Container B and Container C, then put the result into Container A.")
  • Format AD: "Do something involving Container A and a specific number or index D." (e.g., LOP_LOADK R(A), ConstantIndex: "Go to the Pantry, find the ingredient at ConstantIndex, and put it into Container A.")

What some of these specific commands mean for our robot chef:

  • LOP_MOVE: "Copy the ingredient from Container B to Container A."
  • LOP_LOADK: "Get a pre-made ingredient from the Pantry (identified by its number D) and put it into Container A."
  • LOP_LOADNIL: "Make Container A empty."
  • LOP_LOADBOOL: "Put a 'true' or 'false' (like 'yes' or 'no') into Container A. Then, maybe skip some steps depending on the value."
  • LOP_LOADN: "Quickly put a small number D directly into Container A, without needing to go to the Pantry."
  • LOP_NEWTABLE: "Prepare a brand new, organized dish (like a recipe for a cake, which has many ingredients) in Container A. The attached aux note tells me how many slots to prepare in the cake tin right from the start."
  • LOP_GETTABLEKS / LOP_SETTABLEKS: "Get an ingredient from, or put an ingredient into, a specific spot in dish B/A, using a specific label. The aux note sometimes tells me *exactly* where that spot is, so I don't have to search every time, making it faster!"
  • LOP_GETGLOBAL: "Get an ingredient that's always available in the main, global pantry (like 'salt') and put it into Container A. The aux note helps me remember where I found it last."
  • LOP_GETIMPORT: "Fetch a special, pre-made component (like a pre-baked pie crust for math.floor) directly, without looking it up multiple times. The aux note tells me exactly where to find it efficiently."
  • LOP_ADD / LOP_SUB / LOP_MUL / LOP_DIV: "Perform basic math (add, subtract, multiply, divide) with ingredients from Container B and Container C, and put the result in Container A. If B or C are special 'dishes,' they might have their own rules for how to do the math."
  • LOP_CONCAT: "Take all the ingredients from Container B up to Container C and combine them into one long string (like joining all the words together), putting the final result in Container A."
  • LOP_JUMP: "Skip forward or backward D steps in the recipe book."
  • LOP_JUMPIFEQ: "If Container A and Container B have the exact same ingredient, then skip some steps in the recipe."
  • LOP_FORNPREP / LOP_FORNLOOP: These are special commands for repeating a set of steps a specific number of times, like 'stir 10 times.'

In essence, these opcodes are the fundamental building blocks—the simplest instructions—that the Luau robot chef follows, one by one, to execute your complex Luau programs.

sections.title: Section 33: Tvalue
sections.html:
Section 33: Memory Representation (`TValue` Struct)

Imagine Luau as a skilled chef in a kitchen, and every piece of data – a number, a piece of text, a true/false answer – is an ingredient. To keep the kitchen tidy and efficient, the chef uses a special, standardized container for *every single ingredient*. This container is like a "multi-purpose ingredient box."

This special box, which Luau internally calls a TValue (think of it as "Type-Value"), is designed to be super flexible. Every box is exactly the same size, no matter what's inside. This helps the chef quickly grab, move, and store ingredients without having to measure or figure out different box sizes all the time.

Each "multi-purpose ingredient box" always has two main parts:

1. The Ingredient Space: This is the main part of the box where the actual ingredient goes. It's big enough to hold a few different kinds of small ingredients directly.

2. The Label: This is a small sticker on the outside of the box that tells the chef exactly *what kind* of ingredient is currently inside. Is it a number? A piece of text? A true/false answer? The label guides the chef on how to use the ingredient.

How Luau stores ingredients:

  • Small, Direct Ingredients (Numbers, True/False, Small Coordinates): For these common, simple ingredients, Luau puts them *directly inside* the ingredient space of the multi-purpose box. This is super fast because the ingredient is right there. It's like having your salt or sugar in a small, clearly labeled container on the counter. You grab the box, and the ingredient is immediately available.
  • Big, Complex Ingredients (Longer Text, Tables, Functions): Some ingredients are too big or complex to fit directly into the small box (like a whole cake or a multi-layered recipe). For these, Luau doesn't put the *entire* thing in the box. Instead, the ingredient space holds a small "address card" or "map" that tells the chef *where* to find the larger ingredient in a bigger storage pantry (a shared memory area). The label on the box still tells the chef, "This box has a map to a cake recipe." A special kitchen assistant (the "Garbage Collector") helps keep track of these bigger ingredients in the pantry, making sure they're cleaned up when no longer needed.

This clever system allows Luau to handle all sorts of data efficiently, knowing that every value it works with comes in a predictable, easy-to-manage "multi-purpose ingredient box."

sections.title: Section 34: Garbage Collection
sections.html:
Section 34: Tri-Color Garbage Collection

Imagine your computer's memory is like a giant library, and all the pieces of data your Luau program uses (like numbers, text, tables, or functions) are like books. Just like books, these pieces of data take up space.

As your program runs, it creates many "books" and sometimes stops using others. If we never cleaned up, the library would quickly get full of old, unused books, and eventually, there'd be no space left for new ones! This is where the "Garbage Collector" (GC) comes in – it's like a smart librarian whose job is to keep the library tidy.

Here's how this special librarian works:

  • Tidying in Shifts (Incremental): Instead of closing the whole library for a day to clean (which would make your program freeze!), this librarian cleans up small sections at a time while people are still reading. This keeps things running smoothly.
  • The Three-Color System (Tri-Color GC): The librarian uses a clever system to decide which books to keep and which to throw away:
  • White Books (Garbage Candidates): These are books that the librarian hasn't looked at yet, or that seem to be just lying around without anyone using them. They're potential candidates for being removed.
  • Gray Books (Being Checked Out): These are books that the librarian has *noticed* are being used by someone, or are on a cart to be put back on a shelf. But the librarian hasn't finished checking *all* their details yet – for example, if it's a series, they need to make sure all parts of the series are accounted for.
  • Black Books (Safe and Sound): These are books that the librarian has fully inspected and confirmed are definitely in use, either checked out or properly organized. They are safe from being thrown away during this cleanup round.
  • The Cleanup Process:

1. Finding the Important Books (Mark Phase): The librarian starts by identifying the most important "root" shelves – like the main checkout desk, or the "currently in use" tables. Any books directly referenced from these roots are immediately marked "Gray." Then, the librarian works through all the "Gray" books. For each Gray book, they check all the other books it points to (like a "see also" reference). If they find a "White" book, they mark *it* "Gray" too. Once a Gray book's references are all checked, it turns "Black." This process continues until there are no Gray books left – meaning everything reachable from the important roots is now Black.

2. Removing the Trash (Sweep Phase): After finding all the "Black" (in-use) books, the librarian then goes through *all* the books in the library. Any book that is still "White" at this point is considered truly unused "garbage" and is removed. The "Black" books are then reset to "White" for the *next* cleanup round.

  • The Watchful Eye (Write Barrier): What if someone is reading a "Black" book (confirmed in use) and then puts a "White" bookmark (potentially garbage) inside it *while* the librarian is cleaning? We wouldn't want the librarian to throw away that bookmark! The "write barrier" is like a vigilant assistant who sees this happen. They'll either quickly mark the bookmark "Gray" (so the librarian checks it) or mark the main book "Gray" again (so the librarian re-checks it and its contents). This ensures no important bookmarks get accidentally thrown away.
  • Oops, I Forgot to Throw That Out (Upvalue Leaks): Sometimes, you might create a special note (a function) and stick it permanently to the main library notice board (a global table). If that note mentions a specific book (some data), that book will *always* be considered "in use" because the note is globally accessible. Even if no one else is reading it, the librarian will never throw it away!
  • Librarian's Settings (Tuning Parameters): You can tell the librarian how fast or how often to clean:
  • gcstepmul (Cleaning Speed): How much effort the librarian puts into cleaning for every new book that arrives. If this is too low, new books might pile up faster than they can be tidied!
  • gcpause (Pause Before Next Cleanup): How long the librarian waits after a big cleanup before deciding it's time to start looking for new messes again.

This system ensures your program runs efficiently, using only the memory it needs, without you having to constantly track and delete data manually.

sections.title: Section 35: String Interning
sections.html:
Section 35: String Interning Engine

Imagine your computer's memory is like a huge library. When you use words or sentences in your code (what we call "strings"), the computer needs to store them. Now, if you keep using the same word, like "Hello," multiple times, it would be wasteful to make a brand new copy every single time you type "Hello."

That's where Luau's "String Interning Engine" comes in, acting like a super-smart librarian.

1. Unique Copies Only: This librarian makes sure that for every unique word or phrase, there's only *one* master copy stored in the whole library.

2. Requesting a Word: When your code asks for a word (say, "Luau"), the librarian first quickly checks if that exact word is already on a special shelf.

3. Returning the Word:

  • If "Luau" is already there, the librarian just points you to the existing copy. This is like pointing to the one "Moby Dick" book on the shelf.
  • If "Luau" isn't there yet, the librarian creates a new master copy, puts it on the shelf, and then points you to *that* copy.

This clever system has two big benefits:

  • Saves Space: Because there's only one copy of each unique word, your computer's memory isn't cluttered with duplicates. It's like having one physical dictionary where you can look up any word, instead of carrying around a separate copy of that dictionary for every single time you use a word.
  • Faster Comparisons: When the computer needs to check if two words are the same, it doesn't have to read them character by character. It just checks if they point to the *exact same master copy* in the library. This is incredibly fast, like comparing two identical ID numbers instead of reading two whole books to see if they match.

sections.title: Section 36: Native Execution
sections.html:
